agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framing it as an infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes. The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's compliance posture, contractual obligations, and data governance model. Delivery records in logistics are legal documents that serve as proof of service, basis for invoicing, evidence in disputes, and inputs to regulatory compliance reporting.","bounded_context":"Data Governance / Delivery Record Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The canonical record of a completed delivery, including proof of delivery, timestamps, route data, recipient confirmation, and associated metadata. Serves as both an operational artifact and a legal document."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The rules governing how long different categories of data are stored, driven by regulatory requirements, contractual obligations, and business needs — not storage costs alone."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient. Required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and invoicing. Has legal weight in commercial disputes."},{"term":"Right to Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)","definition":"A data subject's right to request deletion of personal data, which is OVERRIDDEN when retention is necessary for legal obligations or establishment/defense of legal claims."},{"term":"Statutory Retention Period","definition":"The legally mandated minimum period for which commercial records must be retained. Varies by jurisdiction and record type — typically 6-10 years in EU/UK for tax and commercial records."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of delivery data by business customers for their own regulatory filings, audit trails, and contractual compliance — a service the platform implicitly or explicitly provides by retaining data."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records must be retained for at least the statutory minimum period required by each applicable jurisdiction (EU member states and UK).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Violating statutory retention requirements exposes the company to regulatory fines, audit failures, and legal liability. This is a legal constraint, not a business policy.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record in any EU/UK jurisdiction, WHEN the statutory retention period has NOT elapsed, THEN the record MUST NOT be deleted or made inaccessible."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery records that serve as proof of delivery must be retained for the duration of the applicable limitation period for commercial disputes (typically 6 years in the UK, varies by EU member state).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Destroying proof of delivery before the limitation period expires eliminates the company's and its customers' ability to defend against claims. This is a legal and contractual integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery with an associated proof of delivery, WHEN the limitation period for disputes has NOT expired, THEN the POD and associated delivery record MUST be retained."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customer delivery data must be retained for at least the period specified in their service agreements or for the period required by their compliance reporting obligations, whichever is longer.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a contractual obligation that could theoretically be renegotiated, but unilateral deletion would constitute breach of contract.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a B2B customer with a contractual data retention requirement of N years, WHEN a delivery record is younger than N years, THEN the record MUST NOT be auto-deleted."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Tax-relevant delivery records (invoicing, VAT, customs declarations) must be retained for the period required by applicable tax authorities — minimum 6 years UK (HMRC), 10 years in some EU jurisdictions.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Tax authorities can audit historical periods. Destroying records within the statutory window constitutes a criminal offense in some jurisdictions.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record with tax-relevant data, WHEN the tax authority's retention period has NOT elapsed, THEN the record MUST be retained in a form admissible for audit."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Any change to data retention policy must undergo legal review for each jurisdiction before implementation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Data retention in EU/UK is governed by overlapping regulatory frameworks (GDPR, national tax law, commercial codes, eIDAS). Engineering cannot unilaterally determine compliant retention periods.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a proposed retention policy change, WHEN no legal review has been completed for all operating jurisdictions, THEN the change MUST NOT be deployed to production."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"The platform operates in EU and UK markets with statutory retention obligations","when":"Any automated data deletion policy is proposed","then":"A jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction legal review must be completed and documented BEFORE implementation begins"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"B2B customers rely on delivery data for their compliance reporting","when":"Retention periods are shortened","then":"All affected B2B customers must be notified and contractual obligations must be verified as satisfied"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Delivery records contain tax-relevant data (VAT, customs, invoicing)","when":"Auto-deletion is applied to records younger than the maximum statutory retention period across all jurisdictions","then":"The system must prevent deletion and log a compliance hold on those records"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A delivery record is older than ALL applicable retention requirements (statutory, contractual, dispute limitation)","when":"The retention check runs","then":"The record may be archived or deleted, and the action must be logged for audit purposes"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"GDPR right-to-erasure request is received for personal data in delivery records","when":"The record is still within a statutory retention period","then":"Personal data may be pseudonymized but the record itself must be retained for compliance purposes"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A B2B customer requests delivery data from 18 months ago for a regulatory audit","expected_behavior":"Data must be available. Under 90-day deletion, this data would be destroyed, causing the customer to fail their audit and the platform to breach its service agreement."},{"scenario":"A consumer disputes a delivery after 85 days, and resolution takes 30+ days","expected_behavior":"The delivery record must remain available throughout the dispute resolution process. Auto-deletion at 90 days would destroy evidence mid-dispute."},{"scenario":"HMRC or an EU tax authority audits the company's records for a period 3 years ago","expected_behavior":"All delivery records with tax relevance must be producible. Under 90-day deletion, the company would be unable to comply with the audit."},{"scenario":"Cross-border delivery involving customs declarations in multiple EU states","expected_behavior":"Customs records must be retained per the longest applicable retention period among all involved jurisdictions, which may be 10+ years."},{"scenario":"A customer exercises GDPR right-to-erasure on a delivery record that is also tax-relevant","expected_behavior":"Personal data is pseudonymized, but the commercial record is retained. GDPR Art. 17(3)(b) provides an exemption for legal obligation compliance."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE. Regulatory fines under GDPR can reach 4% of annual global turnover. Tax non-compliance penalties vary but can include criminal prosecution. Breach of B2B contracts exposes the company to damages claims. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory fine.","operational":"CRITICAL. Retroactive data loss is irreversible — once records are deleted, they cannot be recovered for audits, disputes, or compliance. This creates a permanent operational gap. Additionally, implementing jurisdiction-aware retention logic is significantly more complex than a flat 90-day policy.","user":"HIGH. B2B customers lose access to data they depend on for their own compliance, likely causing churn of the most valuable customer segment. B2C consumers lose the ability to reference or dispute historical deliveries beyond 90 days.","risk":"EXTREME. This change simultaneously creates regulatory risk (tax, GDPR compliance hold violations), legal risk (inability to defend claims within limitation period), contractual risk (B2B SLA breach), and reputational risk (being known as the platform that destroyed customer records).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B logistics customers","impact":"Loss of compliance reporting data, potential failure of their own regulatory audits, likely breach of service agreements"},{"group":"B2C consumers","impact":"Loss of delivery history and proof of delivery, inability to dispute deliveries older than 90 days"},{"group":"Finance/Tax team","impact":"Inability to produce records for tax audits, personal liability risk for officers"},{"group":"Legal/Compliance team","impact":"Inability to defend against claims, loss of evidence within limitation periods"},{"group":"Engineering team","impact":"False framing as 'no business logic changes' — implementing compliant retention is actually MORE complex than a flat delete"},{"group":"Company leadership","impact":"Exposure to regulatory fines, criminal liability in some jurisdictions for deliberate destruction of tax records"}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"Confidence is low because no legal review has been provided for any of the operating jurisdictions. While domain expertise strongly indicates that 90-day retention violates statutory requirements in EU/UK, the exact retention periods vary by jurisdiction, record type, and customer contract. Without legal counsel's explicit input on jurisdiction-specific requirements, the precise minimum retention periods cannot be stated definitively. The core conclusion — that 90 days is grossly insufficient — is high confidence; the exact compliant policy cannot be defined without legal input.","high_confidence_areas":["90-day retention violates UK HMRC requirements (6 years minimum for tax records)","90-day retention is incompatible with standard commercial dispute limitation periods","B2B customers will be materially impacted","This is NOT merely an infrastructure change — it has regulatory and contractual implications","GDPR does not require 90-day deletion; Art. 17(3) explicitly exempts legal obligation retention"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact retention period required per EU member state (varies: 6-10 years)","Specific contractual retention obligations in B2B agreements (contracts not provided)","Whether current 7-year policy is itself sufficient for all jurisdictions (some require 10 years)","eIDAS implications for electronic proof of delivery records"],"assumptions":["The platform processes VAT-relevant transactions","B2B service agreements include data availability provisions","Delivery records include proof of delivery and are used in invoicing","No legal review has been conducted for this proposed change"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"No legal/regulatory review has been provided for any operating jurisdiction. The engineering team framed this as an infrastructure optimization, but determining compliant retention periods requires jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction legal analysis covering: (1) national tax record retention laws, (2) commercial code requirements, (3) GDPR data minimization vs. legal obligation retention balance, (4) customs/cross-border record requirements. This analysis MUST come from legal counsel, not engineering or domain analysis.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance team","Finance/Tax team","B2B customers","Company leadership"],"options":["OPTION A: Halt the proposal entirely until legal review is complete for all EU/UK jurisdictions","OPTION B: Implement tiered retention (pseudonymize personal data at 90 days, retain commercial records for statutory periods) — still requires legal input on tier definitions","OPTION C: Implement data archival (cold storage) instead of deletion to reduce costs while preserving compliance"],"recommendation":"OPTION A — HALT. Do not proceed with any retention change until legal counsel provides jurisdiction-specific retention requirements. Then consider OPTION C (archival to cold storage) as the infrastructure optimization that achieves cost reduction without compliance risk. A 90-day hard delete is a compliance emergency waiting to happen."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"B2B customer contracts and SLAs have not been provided. The platform's contractual obligations to B2B logistics customers regarding data availability and retention are unknown. Unilateral reduction from 7 years to 90 days almost certainly breaches existing agreements, but the exact obligations and remediation requirements cannot be assessed without contract review.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B customers","Legal/Compliance team","Sales/Account Management"],"options":["Review all B2B contracts for data retention clauses before making any policy change","Engage account management to survey B2B customers on their data retention needs"],"recommendation":"Review all active B2B contracts for data retention, availability, and compliance reporting clauses. Any retention policy change must satisfy the longest contractual obligation."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Framing a data retention policy change as 'no business logic changes' is factually incorrect. Data retention in regulated markets IS business logic.","business_rule_broken":"BR-5"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion would destroy tax-relevant records years before statutory retention periods expire, creating criminal liability exposure.","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion would destroy proof of delivery within the limitation period for commercial disputes, eliminating the company's ability to defend claims.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion would breach B2B contractual obligations for compliance reporting data availability.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"}]}

The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's compliance posture, contractual obligations, and data governance model. Delivery records in logistics ar

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this carefully. This is a data retention policy change for delivery records in a logistics/courier platform operating in EU and UK markets. The engineering team frames it as "infrastructure optimization" but this has massive domain implications.

Key concerns:
1. GDPR requires certain data retention but also limits it
2. EU/UK tax and customs regulations require retention of commercial records (typically 6-7 years)
3. B2B customers use this data for compliance reporting
4. 90 days is far too short for regulatory requirements
5. The framing as "no business logic changes" is dangerous

This is a missing_context situation — nobody has provided legal/regulatory review input, and the regulatory retention requirements are critical but never stated by anyone in the scenario.


I'm realizing the core problem here: the team claims this is purely infrastructure work with no business logic changes, but reducing retention from what's legally required to 90 days is fundamentally a business and compliance decision that should have involved legal review. Since nobody in the scenario consulted legal or documented the regulatory requirements, this is a missing_context escalation—the critical regulatory constraints were never surfaced or addressed.
